annotateRules.description: Needle, awl, and sharp implement safety
annotateRules.trigger: sewing needle, awl, quillwork needle, sinew threading
annotateRules.action: ANNOTATE -- needles and awls are sharp pointed tools; keep a secure grip on the workpiece; never leave needles unsecured (use a pincushion or needle minder); in traditional quillwork, the awl punctures the base material (leather, bark) -- keep the supporting hand clear of the awl's path

annotateRules.description: Spinning equipment safety (spindle, wheel moving parts)
annotateRules.trigger: drop spindle, spinning wheel, quill winding
annotateRules.action: ANNOTATE -- spinning wheel moving parts (drive wheel, spindle, bobbin) can catch loose clothing or long hair; tie back long hair before operating a wheel; drop spindles have a weighted whorl that can strike fingers if the spindle drops unexpectedly -- grip the shaft, not the whorl, when catching a dropped spindle

annotateRules.trigger: natural dyeing, plant-based dye, pokeberry, black walnut, plant processing
annotateRules.action: ANNOTATE -- some Appalachian dye plants are toxic: pokeberry (Phytolacca americana) causes gastrointestinal illness if ingested and skin irritation from repeated contact; black walnut hulls cause skin staining and can cause allergic reactions; always wear gloves when handling raw dye plants; do not use dye pots for food preparation

safetyNotes.needleSafety: In textile traditions across all three cultures, sharp tools (needles, awls, bodkins) are always the first safety consideration. The rule is universal: know where the point is going before each stroke, and keep the non-tool hand clear of the point's path.
safetyNotes.dyePlantSafety: Natural dye plants have varying toxicity profiles. Research specific plant safety before working with unfamiliar dye plants. Pokeberry is the highest-risk common Appalachian dye plant -- handle with gloves and never ingest any part of the plant.
